title: How to Use Rate My Professor Without Getting Tricked by Angry Reviews
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You are registering for classes. You look up a professor on Rate My Professor and see a 2.3 overall score. A chili pepper icon. A review in all caps that says AVOID THIS PROFESSOR. You panic and switch sections. But was that the right call? Online professor reviews are useful, but only if you know how to read them. A loud minority of angry students can make a good professor look terrible. Here is how to filter out the noise and find the signal.

Ignore the Overall Score

The overall score on Rate My Professor is an average of every review ever submitted. It does not distinguish between students who showed up, did the work, and had a legitimate complaint, and students who failed because they never opened the textbook and want someone to blame. A 2.5 could mean the professor is genuinely bad at teaching. It could also mean the professor teaches a required introductory course that many students find difficult. The score alone tells you nothing useful.

Instead of looking at the number, look at the distribution. Are there fifty reviews clustered around 4.0 and five reviews at 1.0? Those five are probably students with a grudge. Are the reviews evenly split between very high and very low? That pattern often indicates a professor who is excellent for some students and terrible for others, usually depending on learning style. Read the reviews to understand the split.

Filter Out the Revenge Reviews

Reviews written by students who failed or withdrew from the course are easy to identify. Look for these red flags: the review is in all capital letters, the review does not mention anything specific about teaching style or course content, the review focuses on how unfair the grading was without explaining what the grading consisted of, or the review contains personal attacks on the professor's character or appearance. These reviews are emotional venting. Skip them.

Similarly, be skeptical of reviews that are overly glowing without specifics. Every professor has at least one student who loved them because the class was easy, not because the teaching was good. A review that says BEST PROFESSOR EVER with no further detail is just as unhelpful as an angry rant.

Look for Patterns in the Middle

The most valuable reviews are the three-star reviews. These students usually passed the course. They are not writing from extreme emotion. They often describe specific aspects of the professor's teaching: what worked, what did not, what you need to do to succeed. Read the middle reviews first. Look for patterns.

If five different three-star reviews mention that the professor is disorganized and lectures are hard to follow, that is probably accurate. If multiple reviews mention that the textbook is essential for exams, believe them and buy the textbook. If several reviews note that the professor is tough but fair and you will learn a lot if you put in the work, that is actually a strong endorsement.

Pay Attention to the Course, Not Just the Professor

A professor who gets excellent reviews for an upper-level seminar might get harsh reviews for a large introductory lecture. The same person can be a different teacher in a different context. Make sure the reviews you are reading are for the specific course you are taking. Reviews for a different course taught by the same professor are only somewhat useful.

Cross-Reference With Official Evaluations

Many universities publish official course evaluations online. These are typically more representative than Rate My Professor because they survey every student in the class, not just those motivated enough to leave a public review. If your university makes these available, check them alongside the informal reviews. The combination gives you a more complete picture.

Know What the Chili Pepper Actually Means

The chili pepper icon on Rate My Professor indicates that students rated the professor as physically attractive. That is all it means. It has nothing to do with teaching quality. A professor with a chili pepper is not a better teacher. A professor without one is not a worse teacher. Treat the chili pepper as completely irrelevant to your course selection decision.

The Bottom Line

Rate My Professor is a useful tool when used carefully. Ignore the overall score. Filter out revenge reviews and vague praise. Read the three-star reviews for specific, balanced information. Pay attention to course-specific feedback. Cross-reference with official evaluations when possible. A professor with mixed reviews who challenges you to learn might be a better choice than a professor with perfect reviews who makes the class easy. You are there to learn, not just to collect a grade.
